title: Claude Code and Claude Agent SDK Workflows

blocks.html:
Claude Code is the agentic coding tool; the Claude Agent SDK exposes the same machinery — agent loop, built-in tools, hooks, sessions, subagents, permissions, MCP — for building your own agents. This is the guide's biggest section because it's a toolbox, and the exam tests whether you grab the right tool.

The mental model that organizes everything: context vs enforcement. CLAUDE.md files, rules, and auto memory are context — the model reads them and tries to comply, with no guarantee. Hooks and permissions are enforcement — code that runs regardless of what the model decides. "Never edit generated files" written in CLAUDE.md is a hope; the same rule as a PreToolUse hook or permissions.deny entry is a fact.

The four soft/hard mechanisms map cleanly: every-session facts → CLAUDE.md; area-specific conventions → .claude/rules/ with paths: globs (loads only when matching files are read); deliberate multi-step procedures → skills or slash commands; must-always-hold rules → hooks.

You want to compare two implementations of a feature from the same starting point. Sessions store conversation, not files — so you need both halves: --fork-session gives each approach its own transcript branched from the shared state, and separate git worktrees give each its own files. Fork without worktrees and both attempts edit one checkout; worktrees without forking and the transcripts tangle. (Related flags: --continue grabs the latest conversation here — dangerous in a multi-task directory; --resume lets you pick; --session-id pins a UUID for automation.)

Details the exam reaches for:

  • Loading mechanics: working-dir + ancestor CLAUDE.md load fully at launch; subdirectory ones load on demand; @imports expand at launch (organizational, not token-saving). /memory is the first diagnostic when rules seem ignored — confirm the file loads before rewording it; the InstructionsLoaded hook logs the details.
  • Auto memory: first 200 lines / 25KB of MEMORY.md load at start; topic files on demand; machine-local, shared across the repo's worktrees.
  • Task-scoped vs path-scoped: a release checklist is task-scoped (skill/slash command), not path-scoped — a paths-glob approximation fires whenever those files are touched, release or not.
  • Skills vs slash commands: skills can self-trigger from task recognition and load their body on demand; slash commands are deliberate human acts.
  • Plan mode vs extended thinking: workflow gating vs reasoning depth — different problems, combinable.
  • Self-review: the session that wrote code is biased by its own reasoning; review in a fresh context, subagent, or CI.
  • Hooks are code: they run with your privileges — vet third-party hook configs like any dependency.

items: Memory is context; hooks and permissions are enforcement. Hard rules go in hooks.
CLAUDE.md = every session; rules+paths = area-specific; skills/commands = task-scoped; hooks = guarantees.
Compare approaches with fork-session AND worktrees — sessions don't isolate files.
Rules ignored? Check /memory loading first; wording was never the problem.
Review code in a context that didn't write it.
